title: Tea on Silk
stainChemistry: Classification: Tannin (polyphenolic). Tea contains theaflavins and thearubigins (oxidized catechin polymers) plus tannin polyphenols. Black tea stains more than green tea due to higher oxidation. The polyphenols bind to fiber through hydrogen bonding with amide groups in protein/nylon fibers. On silk: protein fiber — NEVER use Protein enzymes, chlorine bleach, or OxiClean (too alkaline). Tannin pigments bond through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces. Treatment: acidic tannin formula to break initial hydrogen bonds, followed by sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent — safe for protein fibers).
whyThisWorks: Tea gets its color from concentrated plant pigments that bind tightly and darken over time. Silk is a delicate protein fiber — tea pigments bond tightly to its structure. Heat or harsh chemicals will set the stain permanently and damage the fiber. Sodium hydrosulfite attacks tannin pigments without damaging protein fibers.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin carefully and tamp very gently. On silk, use shorter dwell time and lighter touch. The acidic spotter breaks tannin bonds without attacking the fiber. Monitor closely.

2. Apply sodium hydrosulfite solution. This reducing agent attacks tannin pigments without damaging protein fibers like silk — unlike oxidizing agents which can weaken these fabrics. Monitor carefully and flush when the color lifts.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all chemical residue and loosened tannin. Use cool water only.

4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) with fiber conditioner to neutralize and restore the fiber. Follow with a fiber conditioner rinse to restore the hand and sheen that protein fibers lose during treatment. Air dry flat.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — heat damages silk fiber and sets tannin stains permanently
warnings: Using enzyme stain removers containing Protein on silk — digests the protein fiber
Using OxiClean or oxygen bleach — too alkaline for protein fibers
Using hot water — causes felting and setting
Using chlorine bleach — destroys silk fiber

steps: 1. Blot gently with a clean white cloth to soak up as much as you can.
2. Apply a small amount of mild dish soap diluted in cold water and let it sit for 5–10 minutes.
3. Blot gently with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the color fades.
4. Rinse gently with cold water to remove all cleaning solution.
5. Lay flat on a towel to air dry — no heat, no wringing.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two treatment attempts, take it to a professional. Silk is too delicate to risk aggressive home treatments.
warnings: Never wring or twist silk — gently press out moisture between towels.
Test on an inside seam or hidden area first — silk can react unpredictably.
Never use bleach, enzyme cleaners, or hot water on silk.

neverDo: Use enzyme stain removers containing Protein — digests the protein fiber
Use OxiClean or oxygen bleach — too alkaline for protein fibers
Use hot water — causes felting and setting
Use chlorine bleach — destroys silk fiber
fiberSensitivities: Silk is a protein fiber — enzymes digest it. Safe agents: NSD, mild acetic acid, sodium hydrosulfite. Avoid H₂O₂ — can cause yellowing or discoloration on silk. Test dyes first. Never wring.

escalation.when: Home treatment does not fully remove the stain after two gentle attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Tea stain on silk, tannin-based. Treated at home with enzyme-free detergent and cold water. Please use enzyme-free chemistry only. Reducing agent preferred over oxidizer.

contextQuestions: Is this black tea, green tea, or herbal tea?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting?
Has the item been ironed or put in a dryer?
What type of silk — charmeuse, chiffon, habotai?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black tea stains more deeply. Tea with milk adds a protein component. Dryer or iron exposure makes removal very difficult. Fresh stains respond much better. Thinner silks like chiffon are more vulnerable — minimize dwell times.
